# Cognitive Load Budgeting

## The Physics

Humans have bounded cognitive capacity. Working memory holds roughly four to
seven chunks of information simultaneously. Attention is serial, not parallel.
Comprehension degrades nonlinearly as complexity increases — a system that is
twice as complex is not twice as hard to understand but often four or eight
times as hard.

These are not software limitations that will be patched in a future release.
They are properties of the biological substrate on which engineering decisions
are made. Any system that requires a human to hold more state than working
memory permits becomes ungovernable — not because the system is poorly
designed, but because the operator cannot form an accurate mental model of it.

Cognitive load is therefore an engineering constraint with the same standing as
memory limits, network bandwidth, or processing speed. It must be measured,
budgeted, and respected. Systems that exceed the cognitive budget of their
operators produce errors, slow decisions, and governance failures — regardless
of how elegant the underlying architecture may be.

## Why This Matters

- **API design**: Interfaces with fewer, well-named operations are not just "nicer" — they are safer, because operators make fewer errors.
- **Service boundaries**: Teams that own too many services cannot hold the full system model in mind, leading to blind spots.
- **Documentation**: Good documentation is not a courtesy — it is an external memory system that extends bounded cognition.
- **Alerting**: Alert fatigue is cognitive overload made operational — when everything alerts, nothing alerts.

## Observable Patterns

- Error rates increase when engineers context-switch between more than two or three unrelated systems.
- Code review quality degrades sharply for diffs larger than approximately 400 lines.
- Teams that own fewer, well-bounded services report higher confidence in production decisions.
- Runbook steps beyond seven or eight show significantly higher failure rates during incident response.

## 1970/2070 Note

A 1970 operator reading a core dump could hold a bounded number of register
values in mind at once — exceeding that limit produced misdiagnosis. A 2070
engineer governing a fleet of autonomous systems will still need dashboards
that compress state into human-comprehensible summaries. Compute capacity
scales exponentially; human cognition does not.

## Relationship to Meta Layer

- JOS-P14 (Explicit Boundaries) — boundaries are a cognitive load management tool, partitioning systems into comprehensible units
- JOS-P20 (Acyclic Dependencies) — cycles in dependency graphs defeat mental simulation, exceeding cognitive budgets

### Framework Connection

Framework §2 (lenses bound cognition by showing only L0-L3 per view) and §3 (7±2 cognitive load limit as a design principle) translate cognitive load budgeting from an abstract constraint into concrete navigation and structural rules.
